title: Claude Code Has Interns Now (They're Also Claude)
excerpt: Claude Code can spin up specialized sub-agents to do your bidding. I watched it happen in real time and now I understand what's going on.
body:
I recently asked Claude Code to help me build a blog post — this one, actually — and before it wrote a single word, it hired an intern to read my codebase for it.

The intern was also Claude.

This is the story of how that works and why it's actually a good idea.

## What just happened?

Claude Code has a feature called **subagents** (also called agents). When Claude Code gets a task that's too broad, too risky for its main context, or just better handled by a specialist, it can spin up a separate Claude instance, give it a targeted briefing, and let it do a specific job.

Here's what that looked like when I asked it to plan this post:

1. Main Claude got my request: *"teach me about Claude agents and write a blog post about it"*
2. Main Claude thought: *"I should read the codebase before I plan anything"*
3. Main Claude spawned an **Explore subagent** with a specific prompt: *"look at the blog post format, schema, and any existing posts — report back"*
4. The Explore agent dug through the files, found everything relevant, and returned a summary
5. Main Claude used that summary to write the plan

At no point did the Explore agent know it was working on a blog post about agents. It just had a job, did the job, and reported in. Very professional.

## The lineup: who are these interns?

Claude Code ships with a handful of specialized subagent types, each with its own tools and personality:

**`Explore`** — The one who reads everything and never touches anything. Has access to search, glob, and file reading tools. Completely read-only. You send it in when you need to understand a codebase without worrying it'll accidentally rewrite something.

**`Plan`** — The architect who really likes bullet points. Designed to think through implementation strategy, identify tradeoffs, and produce step-by-step plans. Good for: *"I know what I want, help me figure out how to do it without breaking everything."*

**`general-purpose`** — The all-rounder you call when you don't know what you need. Has access to basically everything. Good for open-ended questions that might require reading files, searching the web, or running commands.

**`claude-code-guide`** — The one who actually read the manual. Specialized for answering questions about Claude Code itself, the Anthropic API, and the Claude SDK. If you want to know how a Claude feature works, this is your agent.

**`statusline-setup`** — Does exactly one thing: configures the status bar in Claude Code. A very focused intern.

## Why does this exist?

Three reasons, in order of "sounds cool" to "actually the important one":

**Parallelization.** Independent subagents can run at the same time. If Claude needs to explore three different parts of your codebase, it can launch three Explore agents simultaneously instead of searching one after another. What would take minutes takes seconds.

**Specialization.** Each agent type has a different tool set and system prompt tuned for its job. An Explore agent can't accidentally edit your files. A Plan agent is prompted to think carefully about tradeoffs before proposing anything. The constraints are features.

**Context protection.** This is the real one. Claude Code has a limited context window — basically its working memory. If the main instance went and read every file in your project, it would burn through that memory fast and start forgetting earlier parts of the conversation. Subagents absorb the noise. They do the messy searching, distill what's relevant, and hand back a clean summary. The main instance stays sharp.

## The mental model

Think of it like a manager/worker pattern. The main Claude is the manager: it understands the full task, knows the history of your conversation, and decides what needs to happen. Subagents are workers: they get a targeted briefing, do their specific job, and report back with one message.

The key detail is that workers **start cold**. A subagent gets only what the main Claude puts in its prompt — no conversation history, no prior context. This means:

- The briefing has to be self-contained
- The better the briefing, the better the result (sound familiar?)
- The main Claude has to synthesize and verify what comes back

## The catch (there's always a catch)

Subagents return exactly one message. No back-and-forth, no follow-up questions. If you brief them poorly, they'll do their best with what they have and you'll get a confident but incomplete answer.

They also can't verify themselves. The main Claude gets a report that says "I found X" — it's on the orchestrator to actually check that X exists and makes sense before acting on it. A subagent that summarizes confidently is not the same as a subagent that summarized *correctly*.

Turns out managing AI workers has the same failure modes as managing human ones: garbage in, garbage out, and you still have to review the work.

## Why this matters for how you use Claude Code

If you've ever noticed Claude Code pausing to ask clarifying questions before starting a big task, this is partly why. The main instance is trying to have enough information to write good subagent briefs. A vague request means vague briefs means vague results.

The more specific you are upfront — what you're trying to do, what constraints exist, what success looks like — the better Claude Code can delegate. You're not just prompting Claude. You're briefing a manager who's about to brief workers.

And now you know who those workers are.
